item.title: Re: Image source
item.content:encoded: Because of this, the image has been removed.

item.title: Image source
item.content:encoded:
that's a Rolex from Floyd Mayweather Jr's collection

http://www.rucuss.com/floyd-mayweather-buys-his-fiance-shantel-a-diamond-rolex-to-match-huge-engagement-ring-photos/ (published a month before this article was written)

I don't know if Rucuss took the pic, but it's clearly not Creative Commons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man…if that becomes an official K-class scenario, I wonder how many people would use it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always knew the prices of ROLEX were a keter class SCP, eg. end of the economic world phenomenon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't even notice that the first time around. That just raised my opinion on this, because damn. You'd need to get someone else to remove the watch for you just to stop its effects… ugh. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One piece of fridge logic that I like about this: the wearer can't remove the watch, because he thinks he already has.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: nice reversal of the story :D thats awesome

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damnit Roget from the past, you just don't get anything, do you?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how such a mundane effect can lead to such disgusting consequences. Only one gripe, though. It says that the innermost layer of his pants were stained with [REDACTED], but unless I'm being an idiot, the redacted substance is clearly pee. Why would the Foundation redact that? They deal with grosser stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Take a note from Lireci here children. The internet is serious business.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really simple effect, initial thought was 'So what?' - but the discovery log explains what. Eeeesh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The watch does not match any known Rolex model.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last bit of the addendum. :|

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The watch in the picture is a woman's watch, and doesn't have the build quality of even a low-grade Rolex.
Also, if you could afford a 22k Rolex, you could certainly afford one with a mechanical movement instead of quartz.
No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs a name on the list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(lit. translation: A clock suited to a king)
(including seven pairs of socks, six pairs of gloves, seven scarves, two coats, seventeen shirts, six pairs of trousers and fourteen pairs of underwear.)

I think these should go into footnotes, for sake of brevity and flow. Otherwise, great article. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would they start thinking you're removing their skin once you got past the layer they acknowledge?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Careful, if you don't watch your language you might be promoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're a fucking moron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 3 litres of watre

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted when I realized what the redacted stains were. Ewww.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm curious about shoes. It's one thing to layer clothing, but you can't really layer shoes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While /jewellry/ is technically correct spelling, /jewelry/ is more commonly used, and more importantly, is what is used on the site. Please don't revert that change again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you should do it.

As it stands, the concept is elegant in its simplicity, but the full implications of the effect are mostly unexplored. I think expanding the article might help.

Neutral vote until then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As much as I would love to add to this, I feel it would dilute the original concept. However, the fact that you inferred some form of "oh dear what if.." reaction means I must be doing this right!

The problem with making the clothes themselves become SCP type objects as an extension is too far-fetched - plus there are troves of evil clothes on the wiki.

To answer both your question and the one above; the wearer would see themselves becoming undressed, only to see clothes underneath. This is because I explicitly stated that the wearer is subject to the illusion when they make the decision to remove their clothing. It would probably cause a huge mindfuck.

Thank you for your interesting suggestion, though!

For the sake of extending my article, I could add in a test log in which they test this very hypothesis, only for the subject to believe that their skin and flesh is being removed, not the clothes. That would be rather horrifying, mostly for the subject. (I'm still not totally settled on this)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A new twist on a classic story. +1

And @platewurm - That is an interesting concept, though it would have to be handled carefully to avoid making it more ridiculous. Though I think that's why it says anyone wearing the watch is to be subdued, and the watch removed: break its hold before you start to remove clothes, because something bad happens if you do it the other way around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this "reverse emperor's new clothes" idea. Maybe it's a little bland for some, I like it the way it is now.

If you decide to spice it up a little bit, here's a suggestion: what will happen if someone else decides to remove SCP-1525-2's clothes by force?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hahaha, well done. The discovery log turned a meh idea into a tragedy that calls up every at school/work/in public without pants related nightmare anyone's ever had.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think perhaps he meant "hyperthermia", which is the opposite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I laughed, then wondered what the hell I read. This is just too goofy to upvote, though the discovery log is keeping me from downvoting. I mean, at least it clarifies what's bad about this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like the allusion to The Emperor's New Clothes. It seems obvious in retrospect, but I totally didn't see it from the sandbox version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hypothermia?

hypoxia, maybe - skin gas exchange. But hypothermia is excess cooling of body

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have changed it accordingly to be a bit clearer. As Uncandescent explained pretty much perfectly, whenever they attempt to fully undress them-self, they find that they are naked (hallucination) - subsequently adding an extra layer of clothing. This repeats until the watch is removed by another person, or when they succumb to infection/hypothermia/whatever.

Thanks for your interest and criticism!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, that makes sense (when explained, I still think the phrasing is awkward)— but still bugs me. What if they take of a hat in public, or have a rock in their shoe? There might not be clothes to put on, and the SCP becomes more lulzy than eerie.

I dunno, it depends if you want their mental state to be really obvious to others, or more creeping and subtle. I almost prefer the latter, because then it makes the subject's obliviousness less an outright, almost-comical "Emperor's New Clothes" inversion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When they make the conscious decision to get undressed, they will seamlessly think that they are. The intention itself is the trigger.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I got the impression that infection+ asphyxiation+ compressed lungs, ect, could all be contributory causes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this— especially the total mundane-ness of the effect. Definitely upvoted, with a few small suggestions:

Whenever SCP-1525-2 decides to remove any article of clothing, they will immediately assume themselves to be fully unclothed.

Is a key description, and a little confusing as it's phrased. Do you mean after they remove it? All their clothes, or one article? Or … while they're removing something?

I also think it would fledge out the weirdness-horror to list at least a few of the layers the first victim was wearing (like, "ten pairs of socks, of various colors). Humorous and awful at the same time.

Edit: Actually, I'd almost think that "upon waking the next day, regardless of what they are wearing" they'll think they are naked, etc— would make more sense. Either that or "upon fastening the watch", as the variation seems unnecessary/doesn't really add creepiness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is two weeks a realistic time frame for death-by-mold-growth? I assume this was exacerbated by him bathing fully dressed, but something about that bugs me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Forgetting you have clothes on is a fairly boring effect.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. This has a cool oldschool-style effect. Reminds me of SCPs like 018 or Perpetual Circle. Its effect is simple but can escalate to scary proportions, with some imagination.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mould is to Mold as Colour is to Color.

That is, the correct spelling. :V

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get the allusion to The Emperor's New Clothes, but the effect still seems too weak to be a good SCP .
If you added some test logs or an interview that hinted at some darker, more pronounced effects, that could improve it a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will change the description accordingly!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Each to their own! Could you possibly suggest any ways to improve this entry, or is the idea just all round 'meh'?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea, tbh.

Although I counted 22 diamonds. >_>;;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: eeeeh….still pretty lame imo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am grateful for your feedback regardless!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm… I can't tell if I like it or not.

For now I'll +1 it to put it back at 0.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you ever read the emperor's new clothes? It is a similar story, but in reverse. It's not so much that you think you're naked, but attempting to undress yourself causes you to put more clothes on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: its a watch that makes you think you are naked?….what?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, forgot to blackbox that, thank you for pointing it out.

Mould is the English regional spelling for 'mold'. I could change it to the US spelling, but I see no reason to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
relatives of Mr. [ ]

I'm pretty sure you could just blackbox this, or [REDACTED] or something.

were growing mould

Pretty sure this is a typo.

Other than that, not much to say about the article at all. Meh.
